ix 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 
PREFACE 
 
The Biology Form 2 Teaching Module has been developed to provide guidance to the teacher on how to deliver competence-based 
lessons that are grade-appropriate for learners at this level. The Teaching Module is aimed at providing quality education that is aligned 
with the Competence-Based Curriculum and 21st Century Skills. It also aims to facilitate the building of knowledge, skills, values, 
and positive attitudes that are intended to enable learners to live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities 
and society.  
This Teaching Module will help the teacher lead their learners’ transition from Form 1 into Form 2 of the Ordinary Secondary 
Education level in a systematic manner. Thus, the guidance provided in the module will transform the learner from one who merely 
reproduces learnt facts into a creative, critical, and an analytical thinker through selected well-thought out activities which will be 
done during the teaching and learning process. The suggested learning activities are designed to oﬀer learners with hands-on and 
minds-on experiences through the manipulation of real objects, interaction with nature, and learning by doing. Thus, the learners will 
develop knowledge, desirable lifelong skills, values, and positive attitudes needed for their personal, national and global development.  
The module incorporates guidance on how to deliver Competence-based learning approach. The Teaching Module will be of great use at 
fostering learner engagement and practical understanding of biology, rather than knowing and mere rote memorisation of biological 
facts. It is hoped that the guidance provided in this Teaching Module will make learning at the ordinary secondary school level more 
meaningful and enjoyable because it is highly activity-oriented, and allows for knowledge construction and application as a way of 
learning.  
 
 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.)

xi 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
INTRODUCTION 
 
 
 
 
The Biology Form 2 Teaching Module has been developed to guide teachers on how to facilitate competence-based lessons at this level. 
The module aims to deliver quality education that is aligned with the Competence-Based Curriculum which puts emphasis on the 
acquisition of the 21st Century Competences by developing prerequisite knowledge, skills, values and positive attitudes. These 
competences will enable learners to live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities and the Zambian society. 
The Teaching Module oﬀers a guide to teachers as to how they will facilitate the learning in their biology classes through well-structured 
learners’ activities and pedagogies. Therefore, teachers are encouraged to research widely and not only rely on the module.  
At no time is the whole class approach encouraged but rather, the teacher is encouraged to facilitate learning in small cooperative 
groups. It is envisaged that by emphasising cooperative learning, teachers will create a more engaging and eﬀective learning 
environment that will foster not only academic achievement but also essential life skills that will beneﬁt the learners throughout their 
lives.  
This approach aligns with the principles of constructivist learning, where learners actively construct their own knowledge through social 
interaction and collaborative problem-solving. In a case where a learner fails to break through, individualised learning or scaﬀolding 
should be applied so that no one is left behind.

xii 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
PURPOSE OF THE MODULE 
 
 
 
 
The purpose of the module is to illustrate teaching and learning activities that: 
a) are consistent with teaching and learning in a competence-based curriculum, 
b) show processes that allow achievement of 21st Century Skills such as creativity, collaboration, communication and critical thin
king,  
c) demonstrate how biology can be actively taught with the teacher acting as a facilitator of the learning process,  
d) show what activity-based and learner-centred teaching and learning could look like where learners actively learn in small cooperati
ve groups.

xiii 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
HOW TO USE THIS MODULE 
 
This module provides a collection of suggested learning activities and teaching strategies designed to enhance learners’ scientiﬁc 
competences. These activities emphasise hands-on experiences, such as manipulating real objects, interacting with nature, and 
learning through practice. It is crucial to understand that the desired competences and 21st-century skills will not be achieved solely 
by covering sub-topics but are developed through the way teaching and learning activities are carried out. Learners are expected to 
acquire general competences as they interact with the material under the guidance of the teacher. These essential competences include 
analytical thinking, eﬀective communication, collaboration, critical thinking, environmental sustainability, real-world application, 
problem-solving, and the development of essential scientiﬁc skills.  
The activities within this module are intended to be integrated into the teacher’s existing lesson plans, not to replace them entirely. 
Teachers are encouraged to adapt these suggested activities to suit their speciﬁc class situation, learner abilities, and available resources. 
The sub-topics and activities presented in this module align with the 2024 Biology Syllabus.  
Each Concept in the module begins with a “Hook” – an opening statement designed to stimulate learners’ curiosity and motivate them 
to learn. This could be a provocative question, a relevant scenario, or something that encourages learners to think critically. Teachers 
are encouraged to create their own hooks that best provoke their learners’ curiosity and motivation. Consider incorporating problem-
solving scenarios into your hooks. In this Module, you will also ﬁnd the “Content Tip”, which is intended to be for the attention of the 
teacher and NOT the learner, to provide guidance on the learning activities.

CONCEPT 2.1.0: CONCEPTS AND METHODS IN BIOLOGY 
Introduction 
Overview: Concepts and Methods in Biology encompass the fundamental ideas and techniques that explain biological phenomena used 
to study living organisms and their interactions. These concepts and methods form the foundation for biological research and help 
scientists understand the complexity of life. 
Understanding this concept builds the foundation for scientific research and innovation. It equips learners to apply scientific inquiry in 
solving community challenges, while also fostering responsibility for conservation and environmental protection. 
This concept will cover the following sub-topics: Experimental Design, Limits of Science and Scientific Ethics. 
General competences:  
 Analytical thinking 
 Collaboration 
 Communication 
 Critical thinking 
 Problem solving 
 
 
 
 
Hook! Concepts and Methods in Biology can be compared to the magnifying glass through which we see 
the hidden patterns of life. Without them, Biology would just be a collection of unanswered questions.

2 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 Key Concepts/Words/Vocabulary  
Sampling - choosing part of a population to represent the whole. 
Methods - the strategies used to select part of a population from the whole. 
Probability sampling- A technique that ensures that every member of the population has a known, non-zero chance of being selected, 
which minimizes bias and helps create a representative sample. Examples include; simple random sampling, systematic sampling, 
stratified sampling, cluster sampling and multistage sampling 
Non-probability sampling- A technique that is often faster and cheaper but do not give all individuals an equal chance of being 
included, making the results less generalizable to the whole population. Examples include; convenience sampling, purposive 
sampling, quota sampling, snowball sampling and voluntary response sampling. 
Independent variable- A factor that the experimenter changes or manipulates 
Dependent variable- A factor that the experimenter measures or observes. 
Control variables- Factors that are kept constant throughout the experiment. 
 
Sub-Topic:2.1.1: Experimental Design 
Introduction 
Overview: Experimental Design is the structured process of planning how to conduct an experiment in order to obtain reliable, valid, 
and interpretable results. It involves carefully defining variables, selecting suitable controls, and determining how data is collected and

Activity 2: Investigating experimental design using plant growth

Content Tip: 
An independent variable; 
 Is the “cause” in a cause-and-effect relationship. 
 Example: The amount of sunlight given to plants, amount of water given to plants. 
A dependent variable; 
 Is the “effect” that responds to changes in the independent variable. 
 Example: The growth of plants (height, girth of stem, number of leaves etc.). 
Control variables 
 Prevent outside influences from affecting the results, ensuring fairness. 
 
Example: Using the same type of soil, same temperature and same type of plant.

Sub-Topic 2.1.1 Summary 
 Sampling is the process of selecting a portion of a population to represent the whole. 
 Proper sampling ensures results are reliable and can be generalised. 
 Scientific design involves planning experiments systematically. It involves: 
1. Define the problem or question. 
2. Formulate a hypothesis. 
3. Identify variables (independent, dependent, control). 
4. Collect data through observation or measurement. 
5. Analyze results and draw conclusions. 
 Using multiple variables correctly helps students understand cause-and-effect relationships 
 
Sub-Topic 2.1.2: Limits of Science 
Introduction

13 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Overview: Science can help us understand the natural world, solve certain problems, and improve human life. However, it has 
boundaries or limits. It cannot answer questions of morality, spirituality, or subjective meaning. Its methods are limited to what can be 
tested, measured, and verified. In this sub-topic, learners will be expected to explain the limits of science such as laws, language, tools, 
dynamic nature of elements as well as inconsistence of elements; and classifying limits of science such as beliefs, natural laws and 
ecological laws.  
Specific Competence: Identify the limits of science

Content Tip: 
 Science is important for advancing knowledge, but it has clear limits in research.  
 Laws: Science cannot break natural laws (e.g., gravity). 
 Language: Science struggles to explain emotions or abstract ideas fully. 
 Tools: Limited by accuracy of instruments (e.g., thermometer precision). Some research cannot be done because the necessary 
instruments or technology are not available. 
 Dynamic nature of elements: Some elements change states or properties unpredictably. 
 Inconsistencies: Results may vary due to uncontrollable factors. 
 Ethical rules limit scientific research. Some experiments cannot be done because they may harm humans, animals, or the 
environment

1. In a forest ecosystem, you notice that herbivores feed on grass, carnivores feed on herbivores, and decomposers recycle

2. During a school assembly, you observe a group of students bowing their heads and closing their eyes as they begin to pray. 
How should this practice be classified?

ground. What principle does this event illustrate? 
4. A learner claims that science can explain everything, including personal beliefs.

b) Explain why language is considered a limit of science. 
c) Give one example of how the dynamic nature of elements can limit scientific investigations. 
d) State two reasons why inconsistencies may occur in scientific experiments. 
Sub-Topic 2.1.2 Summary 
 Science is powerful in explaining natural phenomena, but it has boundaries.

17 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 Explaining limits: 
o Laws: Science cannot break natural or ecological laws (e.g., gravity, conservation of energy). 
o Language: Some human experiences, emotions, or beliefs cannot be fully expressed in scientific terms. 
o Tools: Scientific instruments have limits in accuracy and range. 
o Dynamic nature of elements: Elements and materials may change unpredictably. 
o Inconsistencies: Results can vary due to uncontrollable factors. 
 
Classifying limits: 
o Beliefs: Science cannot validate or invalidate matters of faith or spirituality. 
o Natural laws: Science must operate within fixed laws of nature. 
o Ecological laws: Science cannot override the balance and limits set by ecosystems. 
Sub-Topic 2.1.3 Evaluate Ethics of Scientific Research 
Introduction  
Overview:   Ethics are the principles and rules that guide what is considered right or wrong behaviour.  Scientific research is guided 
by ethical principles that protect people, animals, and the environment. Ethics ensure that research is conducted honestly, responsibly, 
and with respect for human rights. In this sub-topic, learners will be expected to determine scientific ethics of a given research and 
apply the ethics of scientific research.  
 
Specific Competence:  Evaluate ethics of scientific research

Content Tip 
 
The core principles of scientific ethics: 
1. Integrity and honesty 
o Report data truthfully without fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism. 
o Avoid manipulating results to fit expectations. 
2. Objectivity 
o Minimise bias in research design, data analysis, and interpretation. 
o Ensure fair peer review and publication practices. 
3. Respect for human and animal subjects 
o Obtain informed consent from human participants. 
o Treat animals humanely and only use them when necessary. 
4. Social responsibility 
o Consider the broader impact of scientific work on society and the environment. 
o Avoid harmful applications of research. 
5. Accountability 
o Be transparent about funding sources, conflicts of interest, and limitations. 
o Allow others to replicate and verify findings.

Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Skills Developed:  
 Problem solving by determining scientific ethics of a given research 
  Critically thinking on applying the ethics of scientific research 
 Collaborating by playing specific assigned roles in group work and also respecting the views of other members. 
 Communicating the findings through presentations of the given tasks. 
 Analytical thinking through conducting investigations. 
Expected Standard: Scientific ethics evaluated appropriately   
Assessment 
 
1. A group of Form Two students is conducting a school-based survey on study habits and access to learning resources. The 
survey involves students from different backgrounds, abilities, and genders. The research team must collect, analyze, and 
present findings while upholding ethical principles. You are part of a research team investigating how students at your school 
prepare for exams.  
During data collection, you notice: Some students come from wealthier families and have access to private tutors, while others

CONCEPT 2.2.0: PRINCIPLES OF CELLULAR LIFE 
Introduction 
Overview: The Principles of Cellular Life dictate that the cell is the fundamental unit of all living things, and every organism is either 
a single cell or a complex community of them. To sustain life, cells must perform essential functions such as respiration for 
energy, homeostasis for internal balance, and reproduction to pass on genetic information.  
Under this concept, the following sub-topics will be covered: Micrographs, Staining Specimen and Preparation of Permanent Slides, 
Detailed Cell Structure and Properties of Water. While exploring these sub-topics, learners are expected to gain a range of general and 
Specific Competences through active participation in various learning activities.  
General competences: 
 Analytical thinking 
 Collaboration 
 Communication and  
 Critical thinking. 
 
 
 
 
Hook! Cells are like microscopic cities: they have power stations, factories, highways and even a city wall. 
Without these bustling cities, life would collapse.

25 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 Key concepts/words/vocabulary 
 
Micrograph: A photograph or digital image of an object taken through a microscope to show details invisible to the naked eye. 
 
Staining: A technique used to enhance contrast in biological samples by applying dyes that color specific cells or organelles. 
 
Simple Staining: The use of a single dye to highlight the entire microorganism so that its basic shape and structures are visible. 
 
Differential Staining: A process using multiple dyes (like the Gram stain) to distinguish between different types of organisms 
or cell structures based on their chemical properties. 
 
Special Staining: Targeted staining methods used to identify specific structures that are not revealed by conventional dyes, such 
as capsules, flagella, or spores. 
 
Safranin: A red counterstain used in cytology and histology, most commonly as the final step in Gram staining to color Gram-
negative bacteria. 
 
Crystal Violet: A primary purple dye used in the Gram stain process that binds strongly to the thick peptidoglycan layer of 
Gram-positive bacteria. 
 
Malachite Green: A specialised green stain used primarily to detect endospores, as it can penetrate the tough spore coat with 
the help of heat. 
 
Permanent Slide: A specimen preserved using a mounting medium and a coverslip, designed to be stored and used 
repeatedly over a long period. 
 
Temporary Slide: A "wet mount" or quick preparation intended for immediate observation, usually lasting only a few hours 
before the specimen degrades or dries out.

26 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 
Universal Solvent: A substance (primarily water) capable of dissolving more substances than any other liquid, which is essential 
for the chemical reactions of life.  
 
Surface tension: the property of liquids’ surface that allows it to resist an external force and behave like a stretched elastic 
membrane. 
 
Capillarity: the spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube or porous material, often moving against the force of gravity. 
 
Cohesion: the attractive force between molecules of the same substance that cause them to stick together. 
 
Adhesion: the attraction between molecules of different substances that causes them to cling to one another. 
 
pH (Potential of Hydrogen): the numerical scale used to specify the acidity or basicity of an aqueous solution. 
 
SEM (Scanning electron microscope): a high-powered imaging tool that uses a focused beam of electrons instead of light to map 
the surface of the sample. 
 
TEM (Transmission electron microscopy): a high-powered imaging tool that uses a focused beam of electrons instead of light 
to map the internal detail of the sample.  
 
Light Micrographs (Photomicrographs): Produced using visible light; these often show natural colors or artificial stains to 
highlight specific structures. 
 
Electron Micrographs: Use electron beams for much higher magnification and resolution.

2.1.1 Identifying types of micrographs

 Activity 1: Types of micrographs 
Procedure

 A micrograph is a photograph or digital image taken through a microscope to capture features of a specimen that are invisible to

2.2.2: Staining of Specimen 
Learning materials: Prepared slides or fresh specimens (e.g., onion epidermis, bacterial cultures), stains such as methylene blue, crystal 
violet, safranin, Gram’s iodine or malachite green, microscope, microscope glass slides and cover slips, droppers, distilled water, alcohol

 Activity 1: Simple staining 
Procedure 
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Make all the necessary requirements available for each group  
iii) Ask learners to place a drop of water on a clean glass slide 
iv) Ask learners to carefully peel a thin, transparent layer from the inside of an onion scale leaf using forceps. 
v) Ask learners to place the epidermal layer on a glass slide. 
vi) Ask learners to stain onion epidermis with iodine/ methylene blue/ safranin. 
vii) Ask learners to allow the stain to sit for 1-2 minutes so that the cells absorb the dye. 
viii) Ask learners to place a cover slip over the specimen, avoiding air bubbles. 
ix) Ask learners to observe under microscope. 
x) Ask learners to sketch their observations. 
xi) Ask each group to share their findings with the class

37 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
xii) Consolidate the learners’ findings. 
Content Tip:  
 In situations where microscopes and dyes are not readily available, ask learners to research through videos on internet and then 
make presentations; consolidate their findings. 
 Activity 2: Differential staining (Gram stain) 
Procedure 
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Make all the necessary requirements available for each group  
iii) Ask learners to place a drop of sterile (bacteria-free) water on a clean slide. 
iv) Ask learners to carefully transfer a small amount of bacterial colony on to a glass slide. (Avoid contamination). 
v) Ask learners to spread the specimen into a thin layer to form an even smear. 
vi) Ask learners to heat fix by passing the glass slide through a flame two to three times. 
vii) Ask learners to flood the smear with crystal violet. 
viii) Ask learners to wait 30 to 60 seconds, then rinse gently with water. 
ix) Ask learners to flood the smear with iodine. 
x) Ask learners to wait 60 seconds, then rinse gently with water 
xi) Ask learners to tilt the slide. 
xii) Ask learners to apply 95% ethanol or acetone-alcohol drop by drop until runoff runs clear (usually 5–10 seconds) 
xiii) Ask learners to rinse immediately with water. 
xiv) Ask learners to flood the smear with safranin.

38 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
xv) Ask learners to wait 30–60 seconds then rinse with water. 
xvi) Ask learners to dry the glass slide with blotting paper (do not rub) 
xvii) Ask learners to observe under microscope. 
xviii) Ask learners to sketch their observations. 
xix) Ask each group to share their findings with the class 
xx) Consolidate the learners’ findings. 
 
Content Tip:  
A tour to the hospital/ clinic laboratory would be preferred. Alternatively, videos from the internet would be researched to enhance 
understanding. 
 
2.2.3: Preparing permanent plant tissue slides 
Learning materials: Fresh plant stems (e.g., sunflower), sharp razor blades or a microtome or scalpel, glass slides, and coverslips, 
microscope, 70% ethanol, safranin (for lignified tissues like xylem) and Fast Green (for cellulose), Graded alcohol series (50%, 70%, 
95%, 100%) and Xylene (clearing agent), Canada Balsam or DPX resin.  
 Activity 1: Preparing permanent plant tissue slides. 
Procedure 
Day 1 
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Make all the necessary requirements available to the learners. 
iii) Ask learners to cut extremely thin transverse sections of the plant material using a razor blade.

39 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
iv) Ask learners to place cut sections immediately into a watch glass with water to prevent drying. 
v) Ask learners to submerge the best specimen of the cut sections in 70% Ethanol for at least 24 hours to preserve cell structure and 
stop metabolic activity. 
Day 2 
vi) Ask learners to place cut sections in Safranin for 2–5 minutes. 
vii) Ask learners to wash off the safranin with water, then quickly dip in Fast Green (approximately 10–30 seconds) to create a 
double-stain effect. 
viii) Ask learners to move the stained sections through a series of increasing alcohol concentrations (50% → 70% → 90% → 100%) 
for 5–10 minutes each. This removes all water from the cut sections, which is essential for resin mounting. 
ix) Ask learners to immerse the tissue in Xylene or clove oil until it becomes transparent. 
x) Ask learners to place the section on a clean slide and add a small drop of Canada Balsam or DPX.  
xi) Ask learners to gently lower the coverslip at a 45° angle to avoid trapping air bubbles. 
xii) Ask learners to allow the slide to air-dry or use a warming plate at 60°C to harden the resin and label with the plant name, tissue 
type, and date.  
xiii) Ask learners to view the slide under low and high power.  
xiv) Ask learners to identify xylem (stained red/pink) and phloem/parenchyma (stained green). 
xv) Ask learners to sketch a labeled diagram of the tissue arrangement, noting the clarity and quality of the staining. 
xvi) Make a gallery of the drawings from the groups. 
xvii) Ask groups to walk around the gallery and compare the drawings. 
xviii) Consolidate the learners’ findings. 
Discussion:  
 Why is dehydration critical when preparing permanent slides?

40 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Content Tip:  
 Alternatively, videos from the internet would be researched and then learners present their findings. Consolidate after all groups 
have presented to enhance understanding. 
 Permanent slide preparation begins with fixation to stabilise cell structures, followed by dehydration and clearing to remove 
water.  
 The specimen is embedded in wax for thin slicing and stained with dyes to create visual contrast.  
 Finally, it is mounted under a coverslip to ensure long-term preservation and stability for study.  
 
Skills Developed: 
 Collaborating by playing specific assigned roles in group work and also respecting the views of other group members.  
 Communicating the findings through presentations of the given tasks. 
 Analytical thinking during staining and observation of specimen. 
 Critical thinking through explaining staining techniques on specimen. 
Expected Standard: 
Appropriate staining techniques on specimen applied accordingly.

Sub-Topic 2.2.2 Summary 
 Staining is used to increase the contrast of naturally transparent biological specimens, making specific cell structures (like the 
nucleus or cell wall) clearly visible under a microscope. 
 The types of Staining Techniques include: simple staining (uses a single dye e.g., Methylene Blue to show basic shape), 
differential staining (uses multiple dyes to distinguish between different types of cells or structures). 
 Before staining, specimens must be treated using heat or chemicals (like formalin) to prevent decay and "lock" the internal 
architecture in place. 
 To prepare a permanent plant slide, extremely thin transverse sections of a stem or root are cut using a sharp razor to ensure light 
can pass through the sample.

 For plants, sections are often stained with Safranin (red) and then passed through a series of increasing alcohol concentrations 
(30% to 100%) to remove all water. 
 The dehydrated and stained section is placed in a resinous medium like DPX or Canada Balsam and sealed under a coverslip to 
protect it from environmental damage for long-term study.

Introduction 
Overview: This concept describes the set of biological activities and systems that allow an organism to live and function properly. These 
include processes such as feeding, breathing, blood circulation, waste removal, and reproduction, among others. Collectively, they are 
often referred to as maintenance processes. 
Under this concept, the following Sub-topics will be covered: Nutrition- types of nutrition, Autotrophic nutrition, Heterotrophic 
nutrition and transport in plants.

Key concepts/words/vocabulary 
Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) - the universal energy currency of cells 
Autotrophs - organisms that produce their own food  
Heterotrophs - organisms that consume other organisms to obtain energy 
Producers - organisms (like plants, algae, some bacteria) that synthesise their own food 
Consumers - organisms (like animals, fungi, some bacteria) that rely on autotrophs or other heterotrophs for food 
 
 Sub-Topic 2.3.1 Nutrition- Types of Nutrition.  
Introduction: 
Overview: Nutrition is an important biological process that enables living organisms to consume food to obtain food nutrients for 
energy, growth, and maintenance of life. Since different organisms have various ways of acquiring and processing food, nutrition can 
be classified into different types. This Sub-topic will allow learners to describe nutrition, identify types of heterotrophic nutrition in 
organisms in the local environment, distinguishing autotrophic nutrition from heterotrophic nutrition as well as relating nutrition to 
different organisms found in the environment.  
Specific Competence: Categorise types of nutrition in living organisms

Activity 1: Testing a leaf for starch 
Procedure 
i) 
Put learners into small groups of 4-5 
ii) 
Make available all the necessary requirements to the learners. 
iii) 
Ask learners pluck one leaf from the potted plant and place in boiling water for 30–60 seconds.

67

iv) 
Ask learners to place the leaf in a test tube of ethanol and put the tube in a hot water bath for two minutes until the leaf 
turns white.  
v) 
Ask learners to dip the now-brittle leaf back into warm water to soften it. 
vi) 
Ask learners to spread the leaf on a white tile and add a few drops of iodine solution.  
vii) 
Ask the groups to make presentations of their findings with the class 
viii) 
Collaboratively consolidate the learners’ findings. 
Caution: Turn off any open flames before using ethanol, as it is highly flammable.

 What role does iodine solution play in this test? 
 What does a blue-black colouration indicate about the presence of starch in the leaf? 
 How does this experiment provide evidence that photosynthesis has taken place? 
 Why is it important to use a leaf that has been exposed to sunlight for this test?

Content Tip: 
 Light Intensity: High light intensity speeds up photosynthesis, producing more glucose for stronger growth and higher yield. 
 Carbon Dioxide: Higher CO₂ (to an optimum) concentration allows plants to make more sugars, increasing biomass and 
productivity. 
 Temperature: Optimal temperatures boost enzyme activity, enhancing photosynthesis and growth; extreme high temperature 
denatures enzymes hence reducing yield. 
 Water Availability: Water supports photosynthesis and keeps stomata open for gaseous exchange, ensuring healthy crop 
development. 
 Chlorophyll Content: More chlorophyll captures more light, improving photosynthesis efficiency and crop yield.

Sub-Topic 2. 3.3: Heterotrophic Nutrition 
Introduction 
Overview: Heterotrophic nutrition is the mode of nutrition in which organisms depend on other organisms for food, as they cannot 
manufacture their own food. This Sub-topic will allow learners to identify different types of heterotrophic nutrition in living organisms, 
illustrate types of heterotrophic nutrition in living organisms, as well as evaluate the need for heterotrophic nutrition to organisms and 
the environment.  
Specific Competence 1: Analyse types of heterotrophic nutrition in living organisms 
3.3.1: Identifying the different types of heterotrophic nutrition in living organisms (parasitic, saprophytic, holozoic) 
Learning materials: Handouts on types of Heterotrophic nutrition/Internet access, markers, manila paper, notebooks and pens. 
Activity 1: Types of heterotrophic nutrition 
Procedure 
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5 
ii) Ask each group research on different types of heterotrophic nutrition; parasitic, saprophytic and holozoic 
iii) Instruct learners to focus on the following during their research:  
 Parasitic Nutrition 
o Identify types of parasites in plants and animals and give examples of each. 
o Discuss how the parasites harm the host while they are benefiting from them.

76 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 Saprophytic Nutrition 
o Identify organisms that feed on dead/decaying matter. 
o Discuss their role in recycling nutrients. 
 Holozoic Nutrition 
o Identify animals that ingest, digest, absorb, assimilate and egest food. 
iv) Ask the groups to make presentations using charts 
v) Consolidate the learners’ findings and correct misconceptions 
Content Tip 
 Parasitic nutrition: Organisms (parasites) live on or inside a host and derive nutrients at the host’s expense. Examples: ticks, 
tapeworms, lice, and some plants like Cuscuta. 
 Saprophytic nutrition: Organisms feed on dead and decaying organic matter. Examples: fungi (Mushroom), some bacteria. 
Important for nutrient recycling in ecosystems. 
 Holozoic nutrition: Organisms ingest, digest, absorb, and assimilate food before egesting waste. Examples: humans and most 
animals. Involves processes like ingestion, digestion, absorption, assimilation, and egestion. 
Discussion: 
 Saprophytic organisms like fungi and parasitic organisms such as tapeworms both rely on other sources for nutrition. How do 
their methods of obtaining food reflect adaptations to their ecological niches, and what advantages or disadvantages might each 
type of nutrition present in terms of survival and reproduction?

Sub-Topic 2.3.4 Summary: 
 Transpiration is the loss of water vapour from plant leaves through stomata. 
 Water is absorbed by roots, transported through xylem, evaporates from mesophyll cells, and diffuses out via stomata. 
 This continuous movement creates a transpiration stream that helps pull water upward. 
 Transpiration is affected by: 
Light intensity: Increases stomatal opening, raising transpiration. 
Temperature: Higher temperatures speed up evaporation. 
Humidity: High humidity slows transpiration; low humidity increases it. 
Wind/air movement: Wind removes water vapour around leaves, increasing transpiration. 
Leaf surface area: Larger leaves lose more water. 
 Adaptive Features in plants that reduce excess water loss

98 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Thick cuticle: Waxy layer reduces evaporation. 
Reduced leaf size/spines: Minimizes surface area for water loss (e.g., cactus). 
Sunken stomata: Trap moist air, lowering transpiration. 
Leaf hairs: Reduce air movement near stomata. 
Succulent stems: Store water for dry conditions. 
 Significance of Transpiration 
Benefits: 
o Maintains water movement (transpiration stream). 
o Supplies minerals from soil. 
o Cools the plant through evaporation. 
o Maintains turgor pressure for support. 
Disadvantages: 
o Excessive water loss can cause wilting. 
o In dry conditions, plants risk dehydration. 
 
Concept 2.3.0 Summary: 
 Nutrition Types: Organisms obtain nutrients through autotrophic (self-made food, e.g., photosynthesis in plants) or 
heterotrophic (dependence on other organisms for food) modes. 
 Autotrophic Nutrition: 
o Carried out by green plants and some bacteria. 
o Occurs via photosynthesis, which has light and dark stages. 
o Requires carbon dioxide, light energy, and chlorophyll. 
o Food produced is stored in leaves, fruits, tubers, and seeds.

99 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
o Helps balance oxygen and carbon dioxide and provides food for economic use. 
 
Heterotrophic Nutrition: Organisms cannot make their own food. 
o Types include parasitic, saprophytic and holozoic nutrition. 
o Plays an essential role in ecosystems and survival of organisms. 
 
Transpiration in Plants: 
o Loss of water vapour through stomata. 
o Driven by absorption in roots, transport via xylem, evaporation, and diffusion. 
o Influenced by light, temperature, humidity, wind, and leaf size. 
 
Adaptations to Reduce Water Loss:  
o Thick cuticle, reduced leaf size/spines, sunken stomata, leaf hairs, and succulent stems. 
 
Significance of Transpiration: 
o Benefits: Maintains water and mineral flow, cools plants, and supports structure via turgor pressure. 
o Disadvantages: Excessive water loss can cause wilting and dehydration in dry conditions.

CONCEPT 2.4.0: CONTINUITY OF LIFE 
Introduction 
Overview: The continuity of life is sustained through reproduction and inheritance, enabling species to persist across generations 
while safeguarding essential genetic traits. Meanwhile, variation within populations promotes adaptation, fueling evolution and 
ensuring biodiversity in the natural world.  
General Competences: 
 Communication 
 Collaboration 
 Analysis 
 Environmental sustainability 
 Problem solving 
 
 
 
Key concepts/words/vocabulary 
 Asexual reproduction: Production of new offspring which are genetically identical to the parent plants, without using gametes. 
 Vegetative propagation: Formation of new plants from vegetative parts (roots, stems, leaves). 
 Grafting: This is an artificial method of asexual reproduction where a stem cutting (scion) from one plant is joined to the 
rootstock of another plant so they grow together as one.  
Hook! Life never stops; it flows from one generation to the next, like an 
unbroken chain.

101 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
 Budding: This is a technique where a single bud (scion) from one plant is inserted into the stem of another plant (rootstock).  
 Layering: This is a method where a branch of the parent plant is bent down and covered with soil until it develops roots. Once 
rooted, the branch can be cut off and grown as an independent plant.  
 Runners: Horizontal stems that grow along the ground and form new plants at nodes.  
 Suckers: Shoots arising from underground stems or roots. 
 Corms: Swollen underground stems storing food. 
 Tubers: Swollen stems or roots with buds (“eyes”) that grow into new plants. 
 Bulbs: Underground storage organs made of fleshy leaves. 
 Rhizomes: Underground stems that produce shoots and roots. 
 Clones: Offspring produced by asexual reproduction, genetically identical to the parent. 
 Sexual reproduction: Production of offspring involving fusion of the male and female gametes; results in genetic variation. 
 Flower: Reproductive organ of flowering plants. 
 Stamen: Male reproductive part of a flower, consisting of anther and filament. 
 Anther: Produces pollen grains (male gametes). 
 Carpel/Pistil: Female reproductive part of a flower, consisting of stigma, style, and ovary. 
 Ovary: Contains ovules, which develop into seeds after fertilization. 
 Pollination: Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. 
 Fertilization: Fusion of male and female gametes to form a zygote. 
 Seed: Structure formed after fertilization, containing the embryo. 
 Fruit: Mature ovary that protects seeds and aids dispersal. 
 
Sub-Topic: 2.4.1 Reproduction in Plants 
Introduction

species. This Sub-topic will allow learners to explain the different modes of asexual reproduction in plants, analyse the advantages and

propagate different plants.

Advantages:

Activity1: Propagating plants through grafting 
Procedure: 
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5 
ii) Make all the necessary requirements available for the groups to use. 
iii) Ask the groups to select a healthy rootstock and scion of similar thickness.  
iv) Ask the groups to make a slanting cut on the rootstock stem.

vi) Ask the groups to join the scion and rootstock so the cambium layers align. 
vii) Ask the groups to secure firmly with grafting tape or polythene strip. 
viii) Ask the groups to label their grafted plant and place it in a shaded area. 
ix) Ask the groups to water the grafted plant regularly and monitor changes.

Week 4 
Strength of union, overall plant health 
 
Week 6 
Growth rate compared to the plant not grafted



Content Tip

surfaces with bear hands, use proper cut technique, keep the graft moist and monitor regularly. 
 Commonly used in fruit trees such as mango, citrus, guava, and apple.

Discussion:  
 Imagine you are a botanist working in a conservation area. You are tasked with explaining to a group of farmers why certain 
crops (like beans and sunflowers) produce fewer pollen grains compared to grasses such as maize. The farmers are curious 
because they notice that their bean flowers attract bees, and then maize flowers release large amounts of pollen into the air. 
Using this scenario, respond to the farmers by: 
o Explaining why insect-pollinated flowers often produce fewer pollen grains compared to wind-pollinated ones. 
o Giving at least two examples of crops that rely on insect pollination and two that rely on wind pollination.  
o Suggesting one advantage and one disadvantage of each pollination method for farmers. 
o Explaining the significance of the bee being attracted by the flower. 
Content Tip:  
 Insect-pollinated flowers produce fewer pollen grains because insects transfer pollen directly to another flower, making 
pollination more efficient. 
 Wind-pollinated flowers produce large amounts of pollen since wind dispersal is random and less targeted.

Content Tip: 
 Path of the pollen tube: 
o Pollen grain germinates on stigma after pollination.

Sub-Topic 2.4.1 Summary  
 
Modes of asexual reproduction: Vegetative propagation - new plants grow from vegetative structures such as stems, roots, or 
leaves. Common methods include; grafting, budding, layering and use of natural structures (runners, suckers, stems, corms, 
tubers, bulbs and rhizomes). 
 
Advantages of vegetative propagation include; increased yields, genetic stability (offspring identical to parent) and faster 
maturity compared to seed-grown plants. 
 
Disadvantages of vegetative propagation include; no genetic variation (limits adaptability), plants are more prone to diseases 
and pests, overcrowding can occur and undesirable characteristics are maintained. 
 
Plant Propagation can be applied in grafting fruit trees, budding roses, layering in shrubs etc.  
 
Sexual reproduction in flowering plants involves the fusion of male and female gametes. Flowers are the reproductive organs 
of angiosperms. 
 
A flower has the male parts (stamen)-filament, anther and the female parts (carpel) - stigma, style, ovary, and ovules. Each of 
these parts has a role in reproduction. 
 
Pollination involves transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. There are two types; self and cross pollination. 
 
Agents of pollination are wind and insects. Flowers are adapted to the type of agent for efficient pollination. 
 
In flowers double fertilisation occurs where one sperm nucleus fuses with egg cell, another with polar nuclei. 
 
After fertilisation a fruit with seeds develops. 
 
Seeds and fruits are dispersed from the parent plant by wind, self, water or animals. Each seed or fruit have adaptive features 
suitable for the mode of dispersal.

129

Sub - Topic 2.4.2: Germination, Growth and Development in plants 
Introduction:  
Overview: Germination is the process by which a seed develops into a new plant. In flowering plants, growth of a plant begins with 
the seed which contains the embryo. In this sub - topic learners will be describing germination in flowering plants, investigating 
conditions necessary for germination, distinguishing hypogeal and epigeal germination in plants, describing growth and development 
in plants, and examining regions of growth using microscopes and micrographs. 
 
Specific Competence: Explore germination, growth and development in plants 
4.2.1: Describing germination in flowering plants 
Learning materials: Fast germinating seeds (maize, beans …), cotton wool/paper towels, transparent glass jars/plastic cups, water, 
labels, markers, notebooks and pens. 
 
Activity 1: Describing germination. 
Procedure:  
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Ask learners to place cotton wool or folded paper towels inside each jar/cup. 
iii) Ask learners to moisten the cotton wool/paper towel with water (not soaking). 
iv) Ask learners to place 2–3 seeds on the moist cotton/paper. Label each jar with the date and seed type. 
v) Ask learners to keep jars in a warm, well-lit area (but not direct harsh sunlight). They should be adding small amounts of water 
daily to maintain moisture. 
vi) Ask learners to observe and record results over a period of one week. 
vii) Ask learners to make presentations. 
viii) Consolidate the learners’ findings after presentations.

130 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Discussion: 
 Relate the observed results with biological processes. 
Content Tip: 
 
Learners to look out for seeds absorbing water (imbibition) observed by increase in size, radicle emerging first (anchoring the 
seed) then plumule growing upward (forming the shoot). 
 
Imbibition triggers enzymes. 
 
The stored food in the cotyledons/endosperm fuels growth. 
 
The emergence of radicle and plumule marks the start of independent growth.

incubator), notebooks and pens.

4.2.3: Distinguishing hypogeal and epigeal germination in plants 
 
Learning materials: Fast germinating seeds (known for epigeal - beans and hypogeal - maize germination respectively), cotton 
wool/paper towels, petri dishes, water, labels, markers, notebooks and pens.

133 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Activity 1: Exploring types of germination. 
Procedure:  
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Ask learners to prepare the petri dishes with a growth medium by lining it with moist cotton wool. 
iii) Ask learners to place the seeds in the moist cotton wool in different petri dishes and label them A and B 
iv) Ask learners to observe and record the results over a period of one week. 
v) Ask learners to compare the results for seeds in A and B 
vi) Ask learners to make presentations. 
vii) Consolidate learners’ findings after presentations. 
 
Content Tip: 
 Characteristics of epigeal germination: cotyledons lifted above the soil surface, pushing cotyledons upwards, and cotyledons 
are exposed. 
 Characteristics of hypogeal germination: cotyledons remain underground, pushing the plumule above the soil. 
 
4.2.4: Describing growth and development in plants (primary and secondary growth) 
Learning materials: Fast growing potted plants, water, ruler, notebooks and pens. 
 
Activity 1: Primary and secondary growth in plants. 
Procedure:  
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Using simple diagrams, illustrate the concepts of primary and secondary growth. 
iii) Make available fast growing potted plants to the learners. 
iv) Ask learners to measure stem length, number of nodes, and stem thickness (optional) at regular intervals for a period of 2-3 
weeks.  
v) Ask learners to label which growth is elongation and which is thickening (if visible later). 
vi) At the end of 3 weeks, ask learners to create a mini-poster showing a timeline of growth phases with photos or drawings.

134 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
vii) Ask learners to make presentations. 
viii) Consolidate learners’ findings after presentations. 
Content Tip: 
 After germination, plants grow through two processes: 
1. Primary growth which occurs by lengthening roots and shoots. This allows the plant to explore new soil and reach out 
to sunlight. 
2. Secondary growth which occurs in woody plants by thickening stems and roots, where the vascular cambium produces 
new xylem and phloem. This strengthens the plant and improves transport of water and nutrients. 
 Under the microscope, distinct zones of growth in roots and shoots can be seen as: Cell division zone, elongation zone and 
differentiation zone:  
4.2.5: Examining regions of growth using microscopes, micrographs (cell division, elongation, and differentiation into phloem 
and xylem and cambium) 
 
Learning materials: Permanent slides/micrographs, microscopes, manila paper, markers and bostik. 
 
Activity 1:  
Procedure:  
i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii) Ask learners to examine the slides/micrographs to locate regions of active cell division, elongation and differentiation  
iii) Ask learners to observe and draw sketch diagrams showing these regions in a root and shoot respectively. 
iv) Ask learners to make presentations. 
v) Consolidate learners’ findings after presentations.

Assignments      
1. Distinguish between cell elongation zones and differentiation zones in stems and roots using micrographs. 
2. Explain how cells change from generic meristematic cells to specialized tissues. 
Skills Developed  
 Communicating findings 
 Collaboration through group work 
 Analytical thinking through comparing types of germination 
Expected Standard: Germination, growth and development in plants explored accordingly. 
 
Sub-Topic 2.4.2 summary 
 Germination is the process by which a seed develops into a new plant. 
 Two types of germination are epigeal and hypogeal. 
 Conditions needed for germination to occur are oxygen, water and suitable temperature. 
 Growth is in two phases: primary and secondary. 
 Zones of growth include cell division, elongation and differentiation.

136 
Biology Teaching Module Form 2-Term 1 & 2 
 
Concept 2.4.0 summary 
 Plants can reproduce without seeds through vegetative propagation. 
 Common methods: Cuttings (e.g., cassava, hibiscus), runners (e.g., strawberry), tubers (e.g., potato) and bulbs (e.g., onion) 
 Advantages of vegetative propagation: fast, identical offspring, useful in agriculture. 
 Disadvantages of vegetative propagation: no genetic variation, making plants vulnerable to diseases. 
 Sexual reproduction in flowering plants involves flowers as reproductive organs. 
 Key processes of sexual reproduction in flowering plants are: pollination and fertilization 
 Sexual reproduction in flowering plants results in seed and fruit formation. 
 Seed and fruit dispersal prevents overcrowding and competition by ensuring wide distribution hence promoting species 
survival. 
 Methods of dispersal: Wind (light seeds, e.g., maize, dandelion), water (floating fruits, e.g., coconut), animals (edible fruits, 
sticky seeds, e.g., mango, burrs) and explosive mechanism (pods burst open, e.g., beans) 
 Germination is a process where a seed develops into a seedling under suitable conditions (water, oxygen, warmth). 
 Types: Epigeal (cotyledons above ground, e.g., beans) and hypogeal (cotyledons remain underground, e.g., maize)
